---
title: "Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR"
chapter_id: "cognitive-compilation-and-semantic-ir"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-07-10"
primary_sources:
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "planforge_compiler_arch"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "treellm"
  - "viea"
  - "cca_project"
  - "moecot_manifest_project"
  - "corbens_best_model_possible_project"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "All five assigned local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; support remains argument until compiler traces, target lowering, localized repair, or artifact validators exist."
  - "Source-noted external comparators now ground program synthesis, compiler IR, multi-level IR, and translation-validation vocabulary; no LLVM/MLIR toolchain, compiler pass, semantic lowering trace, translation validator, source-target proof, or program-synthesis run was executed from this repository."
  - "Protocol schema/fixture validation, synthetic requirement-preservation fixtures, a synthetic cognitive-compilation trace harness, the three-project semantic contract-preservation harness, and finite Lean route predicates are implemented. The new harness checks authority-bearing IR, ambiguity and field lineage, pass/failure retention, semantic obligations, observed repair blast radius, and nine negative cases, but it is hand-authored. No source-plan parser, compiler backend, generated target-artifact validator, repair-locality benchmark, or behavioral comparison has been run for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `cognitive-compilation-and-semantic-ir` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-07-10 |
| Primary source records | `cognitive_compilation`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `genesiscode`, `treellm`, `viea`, `cca_project`, `moecot_manifest_project`, `corbens_best_model_possible_project` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `cognitive_compilation`; supporting: `planforge_compiler_arch`, `genesiscode`, `treellm`, `viea`, `cca_project`, `moecot_manifest_project`, `corbens_best_model_possible_project`; external comparators: `ext_pddl_1998`, `ext_shop2_2003`, `ext_tree_of_thoughts_2023`, `ext_dreamcoder_2020`, `ext_llvm_langref_docs`, `ext_mlir_2020`, `ext_translation_validation_1998` |
| Source loading state | source notes and passage-reviewed local raw cache: `cognitive_compilation`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `genesiscode`, `treellm`, `viea`; public-safe pinned-project source notes: `cca_project`, `moecot_manifest_project`, `corbens_best_model_possible_project`; source notes: `ext_pddl_1998`, `ext_shop2_2003`, `ext_tree_of_thoughts_2023`, `ext_dreamcoder_2020`, `ext_llvm_langref_docs`, `ext_mlir_2020`, `ext_translation_validation_1998` |
| Test state | Existing semantic-atom, plan-execution, trace, and Lean gates remain. `python3 scripts/validate_semantic_contract_preservation.py` additionally accepts one blocked three-project lineage fixture and rejects nine mutations for adversarial paraphrase, omission, positional insertion, authority loss, whole-graph repair, digest-parity laundering, failure-bundle erasure, unresolved ambiguity, and target-requirement loss. It does not parse a source plan, lower a generated artifact, authorize execution, measure repair locality, or promote support. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats cognitive compilation as an architectural boundary and prototype target. The repository validates semantic-atom records, a small hand-authored trace harness, and one authority-bearing source-to-IR-to-target preservation fixture with nine adversarial mutations. It still does not contain a source-plan parser, compiler backend, generated artifact validator, localized-repair benchmark, or measured quality comparison against direct generation.

It follows PlanForge because a scheduled DAG still needs an artifact language. Semantic IR gives each node an addressable obligation before it becomes code, prose, a schema, a proof target, or a tool job.

The compiler is the first layer where a plan stops being merely plausible and becomes inspectable. Its output should let another layer ask: which requirement is this artifact preserving, which authority applies, which validator can reject it, and which repair surface is allowed to change it?

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

A scheduled plan is still too loose unless each unit of work has an artifact language. Cognitive compilation gives the stack that language: semantic atoms, obligations, validators, repairs, and target outputs that can be inspected before they become code, prose, schemas, proofs, or tool jobs.

AI work starts to look less like improvisation and more like compilation here. The system is not just generating an answer. It is lowering intent through typed intermediate forms so later layers can repair, verify, replay, or reject specific obligations. That makes failures local: a broken obligation can be fixed without regenerating the entire work surface.

Compilation gives the stack a handle on partial correctness instead of forcing every review to judge a finished blob. Intermediate structure makes errors cheaper because a reviewer can inspect the obligation, dependency, validator, or lowering receipt that failed before the output hardens.

The intermediate form is where vague intent becomes specific enough to preserve, reject, split, or revise. That addressability is what keeps generation from becoming all-or-nothing and lets the IR earn its place as a routine engineering surface.
:::

## Problem

LLM workflows need intermediate representations that separate requirements, plans, artifacts, repairs, and target runtimes.

Direct generation asks one model turn to hold the user's intent, infer missing constraints, choose an architecture, write the artifact, preserve prior decisions, and repair failures. That collapses several compiler jobs into one opaque act. Cognitive compilation splits those jobs apart: source plan, semantic IR, target lowering, execution, validation, and localized repair.

The layer receives accepted goals and command contracts from planning. It emits typed semantic atoms with dependencies, constraints, validators, target hints, and repair surfaces. The output should be boring in the best sense: a downstream worker can inspect what must be preserved without rereading the entire conversation.

Repair locality creates the second pressure. When a generated artifact fails, an opaque prompt transcript gives the system few stable addresses for what broke. A semantic IR should identify which obligation, dependency, validator, or lowering step failed so repair does not rewrite the whole artifact by accident. That is how compilation turns generation into inspectable work.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Generate-from-prompt workflows entangle requirements and implementation, drift over long contexts, and make incremental repair fragile.

The `cognitive_compilation` source identifies the recurring failure pattern: requirement and implementation entangle, independent work is serialized, expensive models are overused, and repairs become global regeneration. PlanForge contributes the scheduling side of the answer; GenesisCode contributes the evidence/provenance side; TreeLLM contributes the representation-side intuition that the model should navigate explicit structure rather than memorize everything internally.

External baselines sharpen the compiler analogy. PDDL (`ext_pddl_1998`) and SHOP2 (`ext_shop2_2003`) show why action models and decomposition methods deserve explicit intermediate forms, Tree of Thoughts (`ext_tree_of_thoughts_2023`) treats reasoning paths as inspectable candidates, and DreamCoder (`ext_dreamcoder_2020`) shows library learning as compression through reusable abstractions. LLVM IR (`ext_llvm_langref_docs`) grounds the mature compiler practice of typed intermediate forms, equivalent representations, verifier boundaries, and transformation/analysis surfaces. MLIR (`ext_mlir_2020`) adds multi-level IR, dialects, modular passes, verifiers, and progressive lowering across targets. Translation validation (`ext_translation_validation_1998`) supplies the exact missing safety pressure: each lowering should be checkable against its source obligation after translation, not accepted because a generator ran. Semantic IR borrows these pressures toward explicit intermediate artifacts and source-target preservation, not any reproduced program-synthesis, planner, compiler, or translation-validation result.

A prompt can still be the input surface. It should not be the internal build artifact.

The recurring insufficiency is not that prompting is weak; it is that prompting lacks stable intermediate obligations. Once the conversation moves on, the system can no longer tell whether a later artifact preserved a requirement, intentionally revised it, or simply forgot it.

### Strongest objection

A semantic intermediate representation can move ambiguity rather than remove
it. Typed fields and preservation checks may create compiler theater if the
source obligation was misunderstood or the target checker validates only
syntax. The design therefore treats IR lineage as an inspectable repair and
verification surface, not as proof that meaning was captured correctly.

## Core Claim

[cognitive-compilation-and-semantic-ir.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Cognitive compilation should transform goals into semantic intermediate representations that can be optimized, verified, repaired, and targeted.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Cognitive Compilation supplies the compiler pipeline and semantic-atom vocabulary; PlanForge compiler architecture supplies scheduling and tier-routing pressure; GenesisCode supplies provenance, obligation, and effect-boundary discipline; TreeLLM supplies the external semantic-substrate intuition; VIEA supplies the intent-to-artifact ledger that the compiler must feed. The mined CCA and MoECOT Manifest projects deepen the design with explicit ambiguity, authority-bearing semantic fields, pass/failure bundles, and applied repair receipts. Corben's Best Model Possible supplies the decisive negative cases: equal-looking digests are not semantics, positional node identity is fragile, and interface presence is not causal effect. External comparator notes ground prior-art vocabulary, and the local fixtures check record discipline, but none is a reproduced cognitive-compiler run.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C maps this cognitive-compilation claim to eight assigned source notes: five passage-reviewed local raw-cache sources and three public-safe pinned-project notes. The mappings support semantic IR, compiler-style boundaries, provenance, authority and ambiguity retention, pass/failure traceability, localized-repair receipts, and negative cases. They do not establish compiler correctness, arbitrary paraphrase equivalence, benchmarked repair locality, measured quality gains, or a working target-lowering stack.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `cognitive_compilation` | Passage-reviewed: prompt-to-artifact brittleness, Plan Formation, Semantic Compilation, Target Compilation, typed semantic atoms, S-IR/T-IR, compiler passes, DAG scheduling, capability routing, traceable validators, localized repair, implementation-blueprint fields, metrics, and ablations. | No local compiler, benchmark result, trace suite, target-lowering harness, localized-repair result, or empirical ablation validates compiler quality. |
| `planforge_compiler_arch` | Passage-reviewed: orchestration-gap framing, planner-as-compiler analogy, task graph or intermediate representation, typed primitives, quality gates, model/tool tier routing, parallelism, critical-path scheduling, execution handoff, watchdog validation, and source-reported estimates. | Cost, time, routing quality, and artifact quality numbers have not been reproduced here. |
| `genesiscode` | Passage-reviewed: canonical CoreForm IR, structural validation, semantic patch artifacts, stable provenance hashes, sealed effect requests, capability boundaries, deterministic effect logs, replay semantics, obligations, evidence artifacts, registry acceptance, and translation validation. | No implementation, replay checker, proof module, benchmark, translation-validation artifact, or security audit is present. |
| `treellm` | Passage-reviewed: explicit semantic substrates through an external Knowledge Lattice, fixed-width semantic tokens, identity stability, path traceability, append-only updates, externalized knowledge, and path-based navigation. | No TreeLLM implementation, token validation, compression ratio, hallucination result, reasoning benchmark, or graph-coverage result exists here. |
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: intent-to-execution architecture, structured command contracts, artifact graphs, specialist routing, verification, workflow-to-tool compilation, repeated-work closure, claim and verification ledgers, residuals, runtime adapters, benchmark ratchets, and feedback loops. | No completed deployment, working cognitive compiler, execution log, or reproduced benchmark is proven here. |
| `cca_project` | Pinned-project note: Semantic Base Language with typed primitives, explicit ambiguity, deterministic encoding, invocation effects, runtime bindings, and receipts; a five-stage compiler with typed atoms, pass hashes, verification, bounded repair, target IR, and trace bundles. | Project artifacts were inspected but not replayed from this book; source hashes and theorem counts do not establish semantic preservation or runtime correctness. |
| `moecot_manifest_project` | Pinned-project note: authority chain from goals through semantic and target IR; field/provenance/ambiguity retention; pass schemas, strict failure, reproducer bundles, applied localized-repair receipts, semantic portability, and execution-versus-mutation authority. | Broad implementation context only; its compilers, holdouts, and deployments were not rerun, and actual repair locality still requires observed-mutation proof. |
| `corbens_best_model_possible_project` | Pinned-project negative record: rich Task spell and compiler-pass surfaces coexist with keyword-driven parsing, mostly linear plans, digest equality mislabeled as semantic parity, positional repair identities, and ordinary-path defaults. | The project does not establish semantic compilation correctness; interface and artifact presence cannot substitute for causal runtime effect. |

The external comparator notes sit outside Appendix C claim-source mapping. `ext_pddl_1998`, `ext_shop2_2003`, and `ext_tree_of_thoughts_2023` ground planning-language, HTN decomposition, and reasoning-search vocabulary. `ext_dreamcoder_2020` grounds program-synthesis and reusable-abstraction vocabulary. `ext_llvm_langref_docs` and `ext_mlir_2020` ground compiler IR, multi-level IR, verifier, dialect, pass, and lowering vocabulary. `ext_translation_validation_1998` grounds per-translation source-target validation vocabulary. None of those sources proves a semantic-atom parser, cognitive compiler, target backend, LLVM/MLIR integration, translation validator, localized-repair benchmark, or support-state promotion.

## Mechanism

Cognitive Compilation turns planned work into a compiler boundary. Its source plan, semantic atoms, dependency analysis, validator requirements, target lowering, and localized repair supply the main pipeline. PlanForge compiler architecture contributes DAG scheduling and tier-routing pressure; GenesisCode contributes canonical forms, provenance, obligations, semantic patches, and effect boundaries; TreeLLM motivates explicit semantic substrates; VIEA supplies the artifact and verification ledger that the compiler must feed.

This semantic IR pipeline moves from source plan to typed atoms, from atoms to target artifacts, from failed validators back to localized repair, and from accepted outputs into the artifact graph.

The reviewed passages make one architectural move sharper: the compiler is not another prompt template. It is a boundary where obligations become addressable records. Cognitive Compilation supplies the typed S-IR and repair loop; PlanForge supplies scheduling pressure; GenesisCode supplies evidence-carrying acceptance and replay; TreeLLM supplies the intuition that semantic structure should be addressable outside model weights; VIEA supplies the artifact-ledger destination. The current repository now checks a hand-authored trace shape, but it still has to demonstrate arbitrary-goal parsing into S-IR, lowering into real artifacts, concrete artifact validation, local repair under load, and any measured advantage over direct generation.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Human goal + command contract"] --> B["Source plan"]
  B --> C["Semantic IR DAG"]
  C --> D["Compiler passes: normalize, type, lint, schedule"]
  D --> E["Target IR or job graph"]
  E --> F["Artifact"]
  F --> G{"Validator pass?"}
  G -- "yes" --> H["Artifact graph + claim ledger"]
  G -- "no" --> I["Localized repair on failed IR nodes"]
  I --> D
  H --> J["Support boundary recorded"]
  J --> K["Reusable compiler feedback"]
```

**Reading the semantic compiler:** The compiler path moves from goal and contract to semantic IR, then to target artifacts, with validators deciding whether the output can enter the artifact graph. Failure does not restart the whole process by default; it routes back to the failed IR boundary for localized repair.

The compiler boundary represents requirements, semantics, dependencies, artifacts, validators, authority requirements, and target constraints explicitly. Incremental repair then operates over IR nodes rather than re-prompting from scratch. Lowering can produce jobs, code, schemas, documents, or proof targets, but every target artifact should preserve the obligations that made the node necessary.

The key object is the semantic atom. A useful atom names its state, purpose, inputs, outputs, constraints, dependencies, obligation status, authority requirements, IR validity state, validator state, lowering state, target artifact, repair policy, repair ledger refs, source refs, and support-state effect. A collection of atoms becomes a DAG. The DAG is what planning schedules, VCM loads context for, execution turns into jobs, and the artifact graph audits.

A second object is the lowering receipt. A receipt records which semantic atoms produced a target artifact, which obligations were preserved, which assumptions were introduced, which validators passed or failed, and which residuals remain. Without that receipt, a generated file or chapter can look complete while hiding the path by which it lost its obligations.

### Authority-bearing IR and contract preservation

An intermediate representation is not semantic merely because it is structured. It is semantic only to the extent that it keeps the source contract's meaning inspectable across transformations. The minimum preservation packet therefore carries six coupled records:

| Record | Question it must answer |
|---|---|
| Authority-bearing IR | Which principal, domain, operation, and mutation boundary survive lowering? |
| Ambiguity ledger | Which source meanings were resolved, deferred, or left unresolved, and what does each state do to strict compilation? |
| Field lineage | Where did every source field land in semantic IR and target form, and was it normalized, split, merged, paraphrased, or lost? |
| Pass/failure bundle | Which input and output did a pass transform, which semantic validator ran, and which earlier failures remain visible? |
| Preservation obligations | Which source requirements bind which stable IR nodes, target fields, and validators? |
| Repair receipt | Which nodes were declared repairable, which nodes actually changed, and what was the observed blast radius? |

Authority is data in this IR, not an implication of successful compilation. A pass may carry or narrow the authority granted by the source contract; it cannot invent execution authority, mutation authority, or a new target owner. Likewise, ambiguity is debt rather than whitespace. A resolved ambiguity needs a resolution reference. An unresolved or deferred ambiguity must block or escalate any strict route for which its meaning matters.

Field lineage makes omission visible. Every source field needs a source-to-IR-to-target path, including non-goals and authority limits that generative systems often drop as background. A paraphrase is not accepted because it is fluent. Its linked preservation obligation must pass a semantic validator that inspects the relevant target field. The same rule separates content digests from meaning: digest equality can establish byte or declared-input identity; unequal digests can still preserve meaning; neither fact alone proves semantic equivalence.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  S["Source contract"] --> A["Ambiguity ledger"]
  S --> L["Field lineage"]
  S --> I["Authority-bearing semantic IR"]
  A --> I
  L --> I
  I --> P["Pass + retained failure bundle"]
  P --> T["Target contract"]
  T --> V{"Obligation validators pass?"}
  V -- "yes" --> R["Preservation receipt"]
  V -- "no" --> F["Stable failed-node identities"]
  F --> B["Declared repair scope"]
  B --> M["Observed mutations + blast-radius receipt"]
  M --> V
```

**Reading contract preservation:** source fields, authority, and ambiguity enter the semantic IR together. Lowering produces both a target and a pass/failure bundle. Acceptance depends on requirement-specific validators. Repair is local only when observed mutations—not a proposed scope—stay inside the declared subgraph.

The strongest objection is that these records can be self-consistent and still encode the wrong semantics. That objection is correct. A finite validator can miss a human concern, share the compiler's mistaken interpretation, or accept a shallow proxy. The record layer does not solve semantic adequacy. It makes the interpretation, validator, authority path, failures, and residual uncertainty addressable so later empirical or human judgment can challenge a specific boundary. This book therefore treats the new contract-preservation harness as a blocking control against obvious laundering, not as evidence of compiler correctness.

The negative project record matters here. Corben's Best Model Possible called an equality-like comparison over unrelated FNV digest strings cross-stage semantic parity and localized repairs by positional adjacency. Those mechanisms are useful warnings: hashes answer identity questions, not meaning questions, and positional insertion can invalidate repair identity. The book's fixture instead uses requirement-addressed semantic validators and stable semantic node references, then compares declared repair scope with the actual mutation set.

## Interfaces

The interface for Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR is a typed DAG, not a prose plan.

- Planning produces or consumes IR.
- Execution receives target-specific artifacts.
- Verification checks IR-to-output preservation.

Minimum semantic-atom fields:

- `atom_id`
- `atom_state`
- `source_plan_ref`
- `intent`
- `obligation_refs`
- `obligation_status`
- `inputs`
- `outputs`
- `constraints`
- `dependencies`
- `assumptions`
- `authority_required`
- `ir_validity_state`
- `validator`
- `validator_status`
- `target`
- `lowering_state`
- `target_artifact_ref`
- `lowering_receipt`
- `repair_scope`
- `repair_ledger_refs`
- `source_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `residuals`
- `non_claims`

The planner owns graph formation and scheduling. The compiler owns preservation of obligations across lowering. The executor owns side effects. The verifier owns acceptance or rejection. Keeping those roles separate is what prevents an impressive generated artifact from silently overwriting its own requirements.

The boundary should also distinguish four validity questions: whether the source plan is accepted, whether the semantic IR is well formed, whether the lowering is obligation-preserving, and whether the target artifact satisfies its validators. A failure in any one of those states should not be reported as a successful compile.

The public semantic-atom schema now records atom state, source-plan refs, obligation refs, obligation status, assumptions, IR validity state, validator status, lowering state, target artifact refs, lowering receipts, repair-ledger refs, source refs, support-state effect, residuals, and non-claims. That is still only a record shape. It gives later compiler traces a stable place to show which obligations survived lowering, which assumptions were introduced, which validator state blocks acceptance, and which repair ledger must change before promotion.

## Invariants

- IR preserves declared requirements.
- Repair changes are localized and auditable.
- Target compilation records assumptions.
- Authority and non-goals cannot be widened, invented, or silently erased by lowering.
- Every source field has explicit lineage through semantic IR into its target representation.
- Unresolved ambiguity blocks or escalates strict compilation rather than disappearing into fluent prose.
- Pass bundles retain known failure references, and repair locality is judged from observed mutations rather than proposed scope.
- Digest parity is an identity signal only; semantic-preservation claims require requirement-specific validators.

Compiler success is not the preserved boundary; addressable failure is. If a validator fails, the failed atom, dependency edge, target artifact, and repair scope should be visible. If a repair changes a prior obligation, the claim ledger must record that the build graph changed rather than pretending the final artifact still satisfies the original plan.

Obligation non-erasure prevents compilation from erasing commitments. A compilation pass may refine, split, defer, or reject an obligation, but it cannot silently drop it. If an obligation is no longer applicable, the ledger needs a revision reason rather than an absent edge.

## Failure modes

- IR drift.
- Compiler hallucination.
- Repair that breaks earlier requirements.
- Authority loss during normalization or target lowering.
- Adversarial paraphrase that keeps tone while deleting an obligation or non-goal.
- Positional insertion that orphans repair identities or expands the actual mutation set.
- Failure-bundle erasure that makes a repaired build look as though the failure never occurred.
- Digest-parity laundering that relabels equal hashes as semantic equivalence.

The hardest failure is semantic under-specification: a graph can be perfectly typed and still omit the thing the human cared about. The second failure is validator scarcity: prose, diagrams, and policy documents often lack executable checks. The third is overhead: for small tasks, the compiler can cost more than direct generation. Compilation therefore runs only for tasks whose risk, complexity, reuse, or repair burden justifies the extra layer.

Compilation laundering happens when a generated artifact is accepted because it passed a syntactic schema, even though the semantic obligations that justified the artifact were never lowered, checked, or carried into the receipt.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A semantic IR sketch and compile/verify/repair loop for one artifact type.

Cognitive compilation can start with a JSON schema for semantic atoms plus a toy compile/verify/repair loop for one artifact class, such as a chapter section, protocol schema, or small code file. The test should intentionally break one requirement and demonstrate that the repair can be localized to the responsible atom instead of regenerating the whole artifact.

The current schema records one semantic atom shape with atom state, obligation refs/status, assumptions, IR validity state, validator status, lowering state, target artifact ref, lowering receipt, repair-ledger refs, source refs, support-state effect, residuals, and non-claims. The original trace harness adds two passing and four expected-invalid hand-authored records for source-plan requirements, semantic atoms, lowering receipts, target audits, and repair traces. The historical-project closure adds `schemas/semantic_contract_preservation_record.schema.json`, one blocked three-source contract-preservation fixture, and `python3 scripts/validate_semantic_contract_preservation.py`. That harness rejects nine mutations spanning paraphrase, omission, positional insertion, authority loss, whole-graph repair, digest-parity laundering, erased failures, unresolved ambiguity, and target-requirement loss.

This is stronger record discipline, not a compiler run. The fixture is hand-authored, its semantic validators are references rather than generated-artifact inspections, strict execution remains false, and support promotion remains blocked. A compiler claim still needs graph construction from real source plans, lowering rules, concrete target artifacts, artifact-aware validators, repairs over generated artifacts, and measured evidence that repair stays local under load.

A tiny source plan now anchors the first compiler rehearsal as synthetic records: valid fixtures preserve requirements through receipts and target audits, while expected-invalid controls reject a missing receipt, a dropped obligation, whole-graph repair, and a syntactic pass that lacks obligation-preservation audit. The next rehearsal has to connect the same control surface to a concrete target artifact and validator before any autonomous compiler claim is available.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Cognitive compilation reaches product form as a compiler toolchain for cognitive work. Goals, command contracts, plans, and source context lower into semantic intermediate representations before producing code, prose, schemas, proof targets, experiments, or tool jobs. The central product is not the generated artifact; it is the obligation-preserving build graph that explains why the artifact should exist and what it must continue to satisfy.

In a cognitive compiler, every important artifact has a lowering receipt. The receipt names the source plan, semantic atoms, obligations, assumptions, validators, target constraints, authority requirements, repair scope, support-state effect, and residuals that produced it. A later writer, model, compiler pass, or reviewer can change one node without erasing unrelated obligations or pretending that a syntactic pass is semantic acceptance.

A semantic compiler toolchain needs:

- A source-plan language that preserves user intent, constraints, source boundaries, authority limits, quality criteria, and expected artifacts.
- A semantic IR with typed atoms, dependencies, obligations, validators, assumptions, target hints, repair scopes, and support-state effects.
- Compiler passes for extraction, normalization, typing, dependency analysis, linting, scheduling, target lowering, validation, and localized repair.
- Target backends for chapters, diagrams, schemas, code, Lean proof targets, benchmark fixtures, release notes, and runtime job packets.
- Build-system style traces that show which artifacts are up to date, which validators failed, which obligations changed, and which downstream artifacts must be rebuilt.

This obligation-preserving compiler is still proposed architecture. The evidence state should remain `argument` until compiler traces, lowering rules, validators, repair-locality tests, target backends, and benchmark comparisons show that the stack can optimize, route, repair, and rebuild cognitive artifacts while keeping semantic obligations addressable.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Semantic atom fixture validation | Check that the semantic-atom fixture matches the public schema and declares atom state, intent, obligation status, inputs, outputs, constraints, dependencies, authority, IR validity state, validator state, lowering state, target, repair scope, repair-ledger refs, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Requirement preservation test | Check that lowering from source plan to semantic atoms keeps each declared requirement addressable. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_plan_execution_contracts.py`; synthetic atoms only |
| Semantic lowering route proof | Check that a finite semantic-lowering review routes missing source plans, missing atoms, missing obligation links, cyclic dependencies, authority escapes, missing validators, validator failures, missing receipts, obligation loss, invalidating repairs without ledger updates, known residuals, and complete lowering reviews to explicit outcomes. | implemented by Lean build; finite semantic-lowering route coverage only |
| Incremental repair regression test | Break one atom-level requirement and check that the repair can stay within the declared repair scope. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_cognitive_compilation_traces.py`; synthetic trace records only |
| Target compilation audit | Check that a target artifact records which atoms, assumptions, validators, and residuals produced it. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_cognitive_compilation_traces.py`; synthetic trace records only |
| Historical-project contract-preservation fixture | Check authority-bearing IR, ambiguity ledger, complete field lineage, retained pass/failure bundle, source-requirement obligations, observed repair blast radius, and the insufficiency of digest parity against nine mutations. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_semantic_contract_preservation.py`; one hand-authored blocked fixture only, no parser, backend, execution authority, measured repair locality, or support promotion |

The fixture-shape check, finite Lean predicates, synthetic plan-execution harness, cognitive-compilation trace harness, and historical-project contract-preservation harness now cover narrow requirement-preservation records, authority and ambiguity lineage, represented lowering receipts, target-audit shape, observed repair scope, and adversarial negative controls. The remaining tests require a source-plan parser, a concrete generated target artifact, validators that inspect artifact content, and measured repair/quality comparison.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:cognitive_compilation.ir.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.CognitiveCompilation` | A compiled artifact preserves all required IR obligations. | implemented |
| `lean:cognitive_compilation.ir.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.CognitiveCompilation` | A repair that invalidates an existing obligation cannot be accepted without updating the ledger. | implemented |
| `lean:cognitive_compilation.ir.semantic_lowering_route_envelope` | `AsiStackProofs.CognitiveCompilation` | A structured semantic-lowering review routes missing source plans, missing atoms, missing obligation links, cyclic dependencies, authority escapes, missing validators, validator failures, missing receipts, obligation loss, invalidating repairs without ledger updates, known residuals, and complete lowering reviews into explicit outcomes. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over compiled-artifact, repair-acceptance, and semantic-lowering reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: required IR obligations are preserved when a valid compilation review says so, a repair that invalidates an existing obligation cannot be accepted without a ledger update, and a structured lowering review routes known invalid or complete states to explicit outcomes. The synthetic trace harness exercises compatible fixture boundaries, but neither the Lean hooks nor the harness prove source-plan parsing, target lowering, localized repair performance, compiler correctness, or artifact-level validator coverage.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `planforge_compiler_arch` | PlanForge: A Compiler Architecture for AI Task Orchestration | planning_control | Later/alternate PlanForge framing. Prefer highest-quality/latest content after comparison. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `treellm` | TreeLLM | semantic_representation | Hierarchical semantic token system for grounded, efficient, explainable language modeling. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `cca_project` | Compiled Cognitive Architecture project | historical_project_lineage | Typed semantic atoms, explicit ambiguity, pass hashes, verification, bounded repair, target IR, and trace bundles. | public-safe pinned-project note; project runtime not replayed here |
| `moecot_manifest_project` | MoECOT Manifest compiler-era project | historical_project_lineage | Authority-bearing semantic IR, ambiguity debt, field and provenance retention, pass/failure records, applied localized-repair receipts, and semantic portability. | public-safe pinned-project note; compiler, holdouts, and deployments not rerun here |
| `corbens_best_model_possible_project` | Corben's Best Model Possible recurrent-model and mechanism laboratory | historical_project_negative_case | Task contracts and compiler surfaces plus negative cases for digest-parity laundering, positional repair identity, and interface-without-effect. | public-safe pinned-project note; no semantic-correctness claim |
| `ext_pddl_1998` | PDDL: The Planning Domain Definition Language | planning_modeling | External comparator for domain/problem separation, action schemas, and planner-interface notation. | source note available; comparator only |
| `ext_shop2_2003` | SHOP2: An HTN Planning System | planning_htn | External comparator for ordered task decomposition and method-governed planning. | source note available; comparator only |
| `ext_tree_of_thoughts_2023` | Tree of Thoughts | planning_search | External comparator for explicit reasoning-path search and backtracking. | source note available; comparator only |
| `ext_dreamcoder_2020` | DreamCoder | program_synthesis_representation | External comparator for program synthesis, reusable abstractions, and library learning. | source note available; comparator only |
| `ext_llvm_langref_docs` | LLVM Language Reference Manual | compiler_ir | External comparator for typed intermediate representation, equivalent representations, verifier boundaries, and transformation/analysis surfaces. | source note available; comparator only |
| `ext_mlir_2020` | MLIR: A Compiler Infrastructure for the End of Moore's Law | multi_level_compiler_ir | External comparator for multi-level IR, dialects, modular passes, verifiers, and progressive lowering. | source note available; comparator only |
| `ext_translation_validation_1998` | Translation Validation | translation_validation | External comparator for per-translation source-target validation through semantic frameworks, refinement, and simulation-based proof. | source note available; comparator only |

The compiler source set supports semantic IR, representation, provenance, authority and ambiguity retention, and artifact-ledger integration. Five assigned local mappings have passage review, three pinned-project mappings have public-safe source notes, and the external comparators ground the prior-art lineage for program synthesis, planning interfaces, compiler IR, multi-level lowering, and translation validation. The synthetic trace and contract-preservation harnesses provide book-gated record-discipline checks for receipts, target audits, field lineage, retained failures, and repair scope. The core claim stays at `argument` because parser behavior, compiler correctness, real target-lowering preservation, artifact-validator adequacy, localized-repair performance, and quality/cost improvements remain unimplemented or unmeasured here.

## Summary

Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR gives the stack a compiler boundary between intention and artifact. It asks the system to stop treating generation as one opaque act and instead lower goals into semantic atoms, obligations, validators, repair locations, and target backends that can be scheduled and audited.

The practical claim is modest: the stack needs an artifact between intent and execution. That artifact should be typed enough to schedule, validate, repair, replay, and audit. The repository now has a semantic-atom schema plus a hand-authored trace harness, but it still lacks a compiler prototype over concrete artifacts, so the core claim remains design rationale. Compiled work then depends on the virtual context substrate that supplies the right memory and evidence to those atoms.

A compiled artifact should be explainable through its obligations. If the system cannot point from target output back to source plan, semantic atoms, validators, lowering assumptions, and residuals, it has generated an artifact rather than compiled one.

## Handoff

Semantic IR can only preserve obligations when the context feeding those obligations is addressable, authorized, and reviewable. **The Virtual Context ABI: Typed Pages, Cells, and Certificates** defines that memory boundary. It gives compiled work stable context handles, versions, mounts, snapshots, representation contracts, adequacy signals, and fault behavior so semantic atoms do not inherit invisible memory or stale evidence.
